---
title: TI Sitara — AM62x, AM64x, AM243x
description: Texas Instruments Sitara processor family — Cortex-A53 / Cortex-R5F / Cortex-M4F heterogeneous SoCs. AM62x for HMI and edge AI, AM64x for industrial fieldbus, AM243x as full-R5 safety controller. PRU, Linux + RTOS split.
order: 17
updated: 2026-05-13
tags: [vendors, ti, sitara, am62x, am64x, am243x, cortex-a53, cortex-r5f, ethercat, profinet]
---

TI's Sitara line is the heterogeneous-MCU answer for industrial control and edge computing — Cortex-A53 application processors running Linux paired with Cortex-R5F real-time cores running an RTOS, plus Cortex-M4F sub-systems for safety / IO management, plus the unique PRU (Programmable Real-time Unit) for hard real-time IO. The combination targets a niche STM32 and i.MX don't address well: products that need Linux for a user interface or cloud connectivity *and* hard real-time control of motors, fieldbus, or safety-critical IO, on a single SoC.

AM62x (HMI / edge AI), AM64x (industrial communication), and AM243x (full real-time, no Linux) are the current generation. They replace the older AM335x ("BeagleBone") and AM437x families. This page covers the architecture, the role of each core, the industrial-communication advantages, and where Sitara wins versus a 2-chip Linux-SoC + MCU design.

## AM62x — HMI and edge AI

AM62x is the Linux-application-class member. 4× Cortex-A53 at 1.4 GHz, an IMG GPU good for 720p / 1080p UI rendering, MIPI-DSI and LVDS display, dual MIPI-CSI camera input, gigabit Ethernet, USB 3.0, CAN-FD, and one Cortex-M4F sub-system for low-level IO and safety functions.

The defining use case is a sub-$10 Linux SBC class chip that competes with Raspberry Pi RP1 / BCM2835 derivatives, with the advantage of TI's industrial-grade longevity (10+ year availability commitment) and proper functional-safety pedigree. Applications:

- **Industrial HMI panels** — 7" to 15" touch panels running Qt-based UIs.
- **Building automation gateways** — Modbus / BACnet / KNX bridge to MQTT / OPC UA cloud.
- **Edge AI inference** (AM62A variant) — camera + matrix accelerator for vision inference at the edge.
- **EV charge controllers** — HMI + PLC communication + cloud reporting.
- **Medical imaging displays** — high-rel display panels for radiology / ultrasound carts.

The M4F sub-system is what differentiates AM62 from a pure Linux SBC. Connected to the always-on power domain, the M4F runs IO management — debouncing buttons, reading the rotary encoder, controlling LEDs, watching the watchdog — while Linux is rebooting or upgrading. Linux talks to the M4F via shared memory + IPC. From the user's perspective, the UI never freezes during a software update because the M4F is keeping the system responsive.

## AM64x — industrial communication

AM64x drops some of the Linux UI capability (no GPU, simpler display) and adds industrial-fieldbus muscle: four Cortex-R5F cores at 800 MHz organised as two pairs that can run lockstep or split, two PRU sub-systems with hardware support for industrial Ethernet protocols, gigabit TSN switch, and CAN-FD.

The PRU (Programmable Real-time Unit) is TI's secret weapon for industrial communication. Each PRU is a 32-bit RISC core running at 333 MHz with single-cycle GPIO access, no caches, no interrupts (it's polled-loop or event-driven), and direct access to a small block of shared RAM. PRUs run protocol stacks too time-sensitive for a general-purpose core:

- **EtherCAT slave** — sub-microsecond response from PHY edge to acknowledgement, including the protocol state machine. The PRU implements the EtherCAT Slave Controller (ESC) in firmware; with the right PHY (DP83822) and PCB, AM64x is a fully compliant EtherCAT slave at $15.
- **PROFINET IRT** — Isochronous Real-Time mode of PROFINET, where every PLC-to-IO cycle is bounded to microseconds. PRU runs the protocol-specific timing.
- **EtherNet/IP with DLR** — Device-Level Ring, requiring sub-millisecond fault-recovery switching at the slave level.
- **IO-Link master** — multiple IO-Link channels, each running the standard's UART-like serial protocol with strict frame timing.
- **Custom serial protocols** — SPI variants, DSI link to camera, anything that needs deterministic IO.

| Protocol | PRU role | A53 / R5 role |
|----------|----------|---------------|
| EtherCAT slave | Full ESC implementation | Application object dictionary |
| PROFINET RT | TSN packet timestamping | RT/IRT protocol layer |
| EtherNet/IP | DLR ring protocol | CIP application |
| OPC UA Pub/Sub | Hardware timestamping | UA stack |
| IO-Link master | UART per channel | IODD interpreter |

TI provides the PRU firmware as binary blobs ("PRU Industrial Communication Subsystem") for the major protocols; you don't write PRU code unless you're doing a custom protocol. The Cortex-R5 runs the protocol-stack upper layers (TwinCAT for EtherCAT, the PROFINET driver, etc.); the Cortex-A53 runs Linux and the application.

## AM243x — full-RTOS, no Linux

AM243x is AM64x without the Cortex-A cores: 4× Cortex-R5F at 800 MHz, Cortex-M4F, 2× PRU. No Linux, no GPU, no display. RTOS only.

This is the SafeTI-branded part — TI's functional-safety lineage. The R5F cores can be configured as two lockstep pairs (each pair runs the same code on two cores, hardware compares results, mismatch raises a fault). ECC on all internal memory. Built-in self-test (BIST) for the cores, memories, and peripherals at startup and periodically during operation. The result is an ASIL-D-capable controller for industrial functional safety (IEC 61508 SIL 3) or automotive (ISO 26262 ASIL-D, with appropriate FuSa kit and certifications).

Applications:

- **Programmable logic controllers (PLC)** — small/medium industrial PLCs running CODESYS or open-source IEC 61131 runtimes on R5.
- **Motor drives** — variable-frequency drives, servo controllers. R5 runs the FOC loop at 10–20 kHz, PRU drives the gate driver, A53-equivalent (well — R5) runs the human interface.
- **Battery management systems** — multi-cell BMS for grid-scale storage, EV pack-level management.
- **Robot motion controllers** — multi-axis coordination, kinematic transforms, trajectory generation.

Without Linux, the entire firmware fits the FreeRTOS / SafeRTOS / Zephyr model — a single privilege level, deterministic scheduling, no userspace. Easier to certify, lower memory footprint, sub-second boot.

## Heterogeneous boot

The Sitara boot flow is intricate:

1. **ROM bootloader** runs on a tiny secure-only Cortex-M3 ("R5/M3 wakeup processor", depends on variant). Reads the boot mode pins, fetches the signed primary boot image from the configured medium (eMMC, OSPI, SD, UART, USB).
2. **HSM (Hardware Security Module)** verifies the image signature. If invalid, halt.
3. **SBL (Secondary Bootloader)** — loaded into on-chip SRAM, brings up DDR, programs PLLs, releases the first R5 from reset, and loads the R5 firmware.
4. **R5 firmware** boots into RTOS (FreeRTOS / SafeRTOS / Zephyr / TI-RTOS), initialises the peripherals it owns.
5. (AM62/AM64 only) **U-Boot** loaded on the A53, brings up Linux. The A53 sees the R5 as an "AMP" (asymmetric multi-processing) peer — they share DDR but each owns separate cores.
6. **Linux** boots, mounts root filesystem, starts services. RemoteProc framework provides /dev nodes for the R5 firmware images; rpmsg-net provides a kernel-friendly IPC channel.

Total boot from cold reset to Linux login: 8–15 seconds on AM62x. R5 firmware comes up in under a second (it doesn't wait for Linux).

## Real-time and Linux on the same SoC

The big architectural promise of Sitara is "hard real-time + Linux on one chip". Realising it requires care:

**DDR partitioning.** DDR is shared. Linux uses most of it; R5 gets a partitioned region declared in the device tree (`reserved-memory`). The R5 firmware never touches Linux memory; Linux respects the R5 reservation. Boot-time configuration; if you get it wrong, you get either DDR contention slowing the R5 or a Linux OOM.

**Interrupt routing.** Each core has its own GIC interrupt entry. Peripherals are assigned ownership at design time — the EtherCAT PRU's interrupt goes to the R5 that runs the EtherCAT stack; the camera interrupt goes to the A53 running Linux GStreamer.

**IPC.** TI provides the RPMessage (rpmsg) protocol — a virtio-based mailbox-and-shared-ring-buffer transport between cores. Linux side is the standard kernel rpmsg API; R5 side is a TI library. Pass a struct, get a struct back. Throughput easily reaches 50+ MB/s; latency around 5 µs round-trip.

<Callout type="warn">
A common architectural error is putting time-critical loops on Linux with PREEMPT_RT and a high-priority thread. PREEMPT_RT can hit microsecond-class latency *most of the time*, but a 100 ms tail happens — caused by I/O sync, write-back, hugepage migration. For "must always be under 50 µs", the R5 is the only safe answer. PREEMPT_RT on Linux is for soft real-time at the millisecond scale.
</Callout>

## Tooling

- **Code Composer Studio (CCS)** — Eclipse-based IDE for R5 and M4F firmware. Includes GCC-arm-none-eabi, TI Clang compiler, OpenOCD, XDS probe support.
- **MCU+ SDK** — the R5/M4 SDK. Drivers, FreeRTOS, lwIP, USB stack, industrial communication blobs.
- **Processor SDK Linux** — Yocto layer that builds a Linux distribution for AM62/AM64. Includes the BSP, U-Boot, kernel, and userspace.
- **SysConfig** — graphical configurator for pinmux, peripheral init, IPC channels, interrupt routing.
- **TI Resource Explorer** — searchable catalogue of examples and reference designs.

For production, most teams move to Yocto / Buildroot for the A53 Linux side and a CCS or pure-CMake build for the R5/M4 firmware.

## When Sitara wins

Sitara is the answer when you need:

- One SoC running Linux + hard real-time, with both certified for industrial/safety use.
- Built-in support for industrial Ethernet protocols at the silicon level.
- A 10–15 year longevity commitment (TI's industrial parts are guaranteed available for at least a decade).
- Functional safety from one vendor with one DRM, one toolchain, one support contract.

It loses against:

- **NXP i.MX 8M Plus + Cortex-M7 inside** — similar pattern but stronger media (HDMI 2.0, 1080p60 video encode, NPU for vision AI).
- **Renesas RZ/G + RA pair** — for designs that want Renesas's safety story.
- **Pure Cortex-M33 + external bridge to a fieldbus chip** — cheaper for small projects that don't need Linux.

<Callout type="tip">
For evaluation, start with the AM62x SK-AM62B-P1 starter kit ($199 list, often discounted) — gives you HDMI display, USB-C power, headers exposing all the peripherals, and SD-boot. Get a working Yocto image up first, then layer your R5 firmware via remoteproc.
</Callout>
